survey.purpose: Map SO2 Datafication survey responses to comparable BBMRI Directory entities/fields for analysis and possible update-plan generation.

notes: The survey is mostly institution/biobank-level, while the Directory stores both biobank-level and collection-level metadata. Mappings therefore mix exact field comparisons with aggregate/coverage comparisons.
BiobankID is the primary anchor. CollectionID list is a scope restriction below the mapped biobank, not a separate row-level primary anchor in the current data export.
Only mappings marked update_support='current_qcheck' are intended to be safely transformable into qcheck-updater plans without extending qcheck-updater itself.

Institution-level matching should explicitly distinguish exact-ID matches, certain institution-name matches, approximate institution-name matches, and respondents that are entirely missing from the Directory.

entity_resolution.why_relevant: This is the strongest row-to-Directory anchor and should drive matching whenever present.

entity_resolution.survey_field: List of CollectionID in the Directory (if you only represent a part of a biobank; please use ';' as separator in case of more than 1 ID)
entity_resolution.why_relevant: When present, the listed collections define the survey scope within the mapped biobank.
Useful only as a fallback for analysis when no explicit Directory IDs are provided; not safe enough for automated updates by itself.

When the normalized institution name matches one Directory biobank unambiguously, the row can be treated as certainly mapped for analysis. This should still be recorded as institution-name-based rather than ID-based resolution.
normalization: normalize common university/medical-center abbreviations where curated in the mapping JSON

entity_resolution.why_relevant: When no exact institution-name match exists, the tool may still propose approximate candidates for human review. These matches are analysis-only and must never drive automatic updates.

field_mappings.why_relevant: Survey respondent identity can be compared with the linked Directory contact when the row is anchored to a specific biobank/collection contact.
Potential contact-role consistency signal, but free-text role labels differ widely and should stay advisory only.
Email is the most reliable contact-level comparison when a row resolves to one Directory contact.
Institution/biobank naming is one of the main consistency checks for whether the survey row is anchored to the right Directory biobank.
Country is directly comparable and can also detect obvious row-to-biobank mismatches.

survey_fields: Are you interested in promoting your biobank resources to new partners?
Are you interested in promoting your biobank resources to new research or industry partners?
field_mappings.why_relevant: The survey captures willingness to promote resources to academic and/or industrial partners, which is directionally related to Directory collaboration/commercial access flags but not semantically identical.

survey_fields: Which types of samples do you manage?
field_mappings.why_relevant: Survey sample-type selections are the clearest structured source for comparing Directory collection materials; free-text refinements can add coverage notes.

field_mappings.survey_field: What is the approximate size of your biobank (number of samples aliquots)?
field_mappings.why_relevant: The survey gives a biobank-level approximate sample count, while the Directory holds collection-level exact sizes or order-of-magnitude buckets. The tool should compare aggregates and coverage, not single fields.

survey_fields: Do the collections in the Directory reflect representatively the size of your biobank/collections and material types you are processing? (If you only provided biobankID, we assume all the collections of that biobank)
Why are the collections not representative?
field_mappings.why_relevant: This survey answer is an explicit self-assessment of whether current Directory content is representative. It should drive dedicated findings comparing survey claims with observed material/count coverage.

survey_fields: Does your biobank provide access to whole-slide image (WSI) histopathology datasets? E.g., disease-focused cohorts with clinical and/or molecular annotations or normal-tissue reference histology across multiple organs from non-diseased donors?
You can provide more information about available digital pathology imaging data here (e.g., focus of the collections, more details on modalities):
field_mappings.why_relevant: A positive survey answer indicates image-bearing collections should likely advertise IMAGE / IMAGING_DATA and related imaging metadata in the Directory. There is no WSI-specific structured field in the current Directory model, so WSI consistency must be inferred from generic imaging fields and unstructured descriptions only.

survey_fields: Does your biobank provide access to radiology datasets?
You can provide more information about available radiology pathology imaging data here (e.g., focus of the collections, more details on modalities):
field_mappings.why_relevant: A positive survey answer suggests at least one mapped collection should expose IMAGE / IMAGING_DATA and likely an imaging modality.

survey_fields: Does your biobank provide access to other imaging datasets?
You can provide more information about other available imaging data here (e.g., focus of the collections, more details on modalities):
field_mappings.why_relevant: The survey distinguishes other imaging at a biobank level, but the Directory may only expose it through collection descriptions unless dedicated imaging fields are present.

survey_fields: What field of research does your biobank or biomolecular resource support? (Select all that apply)
field_mappings.why_relevant: Research-field answers may expose missing or contradictory thematic descriptors, but the Directory has no direct structured research-field field.

survey_fields: Do you have a policy or workflow for returned data from researchers using your samples, e.g. data transfer agreement?
Please describe your policy or workflow for returned data provide a link:
field_mappings.why_relevant: Returned-data workflows are related to collection governance, but the current Directory access fields are broader and not specific to returned-data handling.
If filled, survey network membership can be compared with Directory network links, but vocabulary normalization will likely require human curation.

In planning / preparation.note: Planning signal only; do not treat absence from Directory as inconsistency.

Yes \u2014 access to WSI data collection.note: May be represented in shared or consortium collections rather than in the local biobank only.

Yes \u2014 access to radiology data coll.note: May be represented indirectly through consortial collections.

Are sample-related data collected in an automated way (e.g., directly from lab instruments or lab systems, without manual input)? Examples include timestamps, temperatures, processing steps, or QC metrics automatically captured by lab devices or software.
Describe the reasons for not collecting (e.g. devices do not provide the information or integration is technically difficult).

Do you track the full chain of custody of each sample and associated data (from collection to analysis for research)? (Select all that apply) This includes documenting the departments, organizations, and individuals (e.g. using coded identifiers) involved at each step, along with time points, locations, and processing or transfer events.
Are sample-related data linked to donor data in your system or on the level of your organisation?

Does your system automatically track changes made to traceability or provenance-related data (e.g. processing, handling, storage, transfers)? This refers to audit trails or version logs typically managed by a BIMS or database system

How would you rate your repository’s technical readiness for integrating full digital traceability? Please choose what corresponds the most to your current situation. By “digital traceability,” we mean the structured, automated, and system-supported capture and linkage of associated data (e.g. preanalytical, clinical, research-derived), enabling traceability of a sample across its full lifecycle, from collection through processing, storage, use, and data generation. This includes the use of dedicated software (e.g. BIMS/LIMS), digital metadata standards, audit trails, and secure linkage to clinical data or research data.
If you require guidance or support to implement advanced digital traceability, please briefly describe what kind (e.g., technical, legal, funding, training):
Does your QMS track processing, handling, storage, transfers of biological material and generated data?

How would you rate your biobank’s Quality Management System (QMS) readiness for integrating full digital traceability? Please choose what corresponds the most to your current situation. This refers to the extent to which your QMS includes procedures, documentation, responsibilities, and controls that enable structured, auditable, and reproducible management of associated data across the sample lifecycle.
If you require guidance or support to strengthen your QMS alignment with digital traceability, please briefly describe what kind (e.g. SOP development, audits, training, templates, funding):
